channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #446
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has confirmed it has no plans to introduce rent controls in England, providing reassurance to landlords across the country. Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner made the announcement this week, stating that ministers would instead focus on strengthening tenant protections through existing regulations.

What the rent control decision means

The rejection of rent controls comes after months of speculation about potential government intervention in the private rental sector. Many landlords had expressed concerns that price caps could negatively impact their businesses and potentially reduce investment in rental properties.

Industry experts suggest this decision could help maintain stability in the private rental market. Without artificial price controls, landlords may feel more confident about maintaining their properties and potentially expanding their portfolios. However, tenant advocacy groups have voiced disappointment, arguing that more needs to be done to address affordability issues.

Alternative approaches to rental market reform

Rather than implementing rent controls, the government appears to be focusing on alternative measures to improve conditions for both landlords and tenants. These could include:

  • Strengthening enforcement of existing housing standards
  • Streamlining the eviction process for problem tenants while protecting good tenants
  • Encouraging longer tenancy agreements by default
  • Improving access to dispute resolution services

Property investors and landlords might wish to stay informed about these potential changes, as they could affect how rental properties are managed in future. The government's approach suggests a preference for market-led solutions rather than direct price intervention.

As the rental market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may benefit from keeping up with regulatory changes and understanding their respective rights and responsibilities under current legislation.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has confirmed it has no plans to introduce rent controls in England, providing reassurance to landlords across the country. Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner made the announcement this week, stating that ministers would instead focus on strengthening tenant protections through existing regulations.

What the rent control decision means

The rejection of rent controls comes after months of speculation about potential government intervention in the private rental sector. Many landlords had expressed concerns that price caps could negatively impact their businesses and potentially reduce investment in rental properties.

Industry experts suggest this decision could help maintain stability in the private rental market. Without artificial price controls, landlords may feel more confident about maintaining their properties and potentially expanding their portfolios. However, tenant advocacy groups have voiced disappointment, arguing that more needs to be done to address affordability issues.

Alternative approaches to rental market reform

Rather than implementing rent controls, the government appears to be focusing on alternative measures to improve conditions for both landlords and tenants. These could include:

  • Strengthening enforcement of existing housing standards
  • Streamlining the eviction process for problem tenants while protecting good tenants
  • Encouraging longer tenancy agreements by default
  • Improving access to dispute resolution services

Property investors and landlords might wish to stay informed about these potential changes, as they could affect how rental properties are managed in future. The government's approach suggests a preference for market-led solutions rather than direct price intervention.

As the rental market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may benefit from keeping up with regulatory changes and understanding their respective rights and responsibilities under current legislation.

item.title: Property tax petition tops 100,000 signatures, triggering Commons debate
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A petition advocating for a significant overhaul of the UK’s property tax system has surpassed 100,000 signatures, prompting a potential parliamentary debate. The campaign, led by the group Fairer Share, proposes replacing council tax and stamp duty with a Proportional Property Tax. This new system would see homeowners pay an annual charge of 0.48% of their property’s value, equating to £1,440 for a home worth £300,000.

The Case for Reform

Fairer Share argues that the current council tax system, based on property values from the 1990s, is outdated and fails to reflect modern housing markets or residents’ ability to pay. The group highlights that council tax arrears have risen to £8.3 billion, with over two million households struggling to meet payments. They also criticise stamp duty for being a “one-off tax” that distorts the housing market and discourages families from moving to more suitable homes.

The campaign emphasises the need for a fairer, evidence-based approach to property taxation, stating: “We believe a tax based on 1990s property values no longer reflects today’s homes or people’s ability to pay — and it fails to provide a stable way to fund essential local services.” Fairer Share is urging the government to commission an independent review to address these issues.

Government Response

In March, the government responded to the petition after it reached 10,000 signatures. It defended the current system, stating that council tax and stamp duty are crucial revenue sources, raising £60 billion annually to fund essential services. The government also highlighted the role of council tax in supporting local authorities, accounting for 53% of their core spending power for 2026-27.

The response explained that properties in England are categorised into one of eight valuation bands based on their capital value in April 1991. These bands aim to establish relative property values rather than assessing each home individually. The Valuation Office Agency (VOA) independently determines these bands, while local authorities set the council tax rates based on their area’s service needs and other income sources.

The government reiterated its commitment to a fair system and supporting homeownership, stating: “Councils are directly accountable to their local communities for how they raise and spend council tax.”

What Happens Next?

With the petition now exceeding 100,000 signatures, it is eligible for consideration for a parliamentary debate. This marks a significant milestone for Fairer Share’s campaign and could pave the way for further discussions on reforming the UK’s property tax system. Homeowners and policymakers alike will be watching closely to see how the government addresses these growing calls for change.

As the debate unfolds, homeowners may wish to stay informed about potential changes to property taxation and how these could impact their finances. With council tax arrears rising and stamp duty continuing to influence housing decisions, the push for reform highlights the evolving challenges within the UK’s property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A petition advocating for a significant overhaul of the UK’s property tax system has surpassed 100,000 signatures, prompting a potential parliamentary debate. The campaign, led by the group Fairer Share, proposes replacing council tax and stamp duty with a Proportional Property Tax. This new system would see homeowners pay an annual charge of 0.48% of their property’s value, equating to £1,440 for a home worth £300,000.

The Case for Reform

Fairer Share argues that the current council tax system, based on property values from the 1990s, is outdated and fails to reflect modern housing markets or residents’ ability to pay. The group highlights that council tax arrears have risen to £8.3 billion, with over two million households struggling to meet payments. They also criticise stamp duty for being a “one-off tax” that distorts the housing market and discourages families from moving to more suitable homes.

The campaign emphasises the need for a fairer, evidence-based approach to property taxation, stating: “We believe a tax based on 1990s property values no longer reflects today’s homes or people’s ability to pay — and it fails to provide a stable way to fund essential local services.” Fairer Share is urging the government to commission an independent review to address these issues.

Government Response

In March, the government responded to the petition after it reached 10,000 signatures. It defended the current system, stating that council tax and stamp duty are crucial revenue sources, raising £60 billion annually to fund essential services. The government also highlighted the role of council tax in supporting local authorities, accounting for 53% of their core spending power for 2026-27.

The response explained that properties in England are categorised into one of eight valuation bands based on their capital value in April 1991. These bands aim to establish relative property values rather than assessing each home individually. The Valuation Office Agency (VOA) independently determines these bands, while local authorities set the council tax rates based on their area’s service needs and other income sources.

The government reiterated its commitment to a fair system and supporting homeownership, stating: “Councils are directly accountable to their local communities for how they raise and spend council tax.”

What Happens Next?

With the petition now exceeding 100,000 signatures, it is eligible for consideration for a parliamentary debate. This marks a significant milestone for Fairer Share’s campaign and could pave the way for further discussions on reforming the UK’s property tax system. Homeowners and policymakers alike will be watching closely to see how the government addresses these growing calls for change.

As the debate unfolds, homeowners may wish to stay informed about potential changes to property taxation and how these could impact their finances. With council tax arrears rising and stamp duty continuing to influence housing decisions, the push for reform highlights the evolving challenges within the UK’s property market.

item.title: Costs force landlords to rethink rents and tenants
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rising costs and evolving regulations are prompting landlords across the UK to reassess their strategies, from rental pricing to tenant selection. According to recent research, a significant proportion of property investors are adapting their approach to navigate the challenges facing the private rented sector.

Rent Increases and Tenant Selection

A recent survey of 200 landlords and property management professionals revealed that 63% have increased rents in response to mounting expenses. Factors such as higher borrowing costs, maintenance outlays, insurance premiums, and compliance requirements have placed considerable pressure on landlords. Additionally, 41% of respondents reported becoming more selective about tenants, focusing on those perceived as lower-risk.

The forthcoming Renters’ Rights Act has further influenced landlords’ decisions, with 59% tightening their tenant selection criteria. Nearly half (44%) are considering accelerating planned rent increases to offset rising costs. These findings suggest that landlords are not only adjusting rental prices but also re-evaluating their approach to tenant risk and long-term tenancy stability.

Cost Pressures and Investment Decisions

Maintenance and repairs emerged as the most significant cost burden, cited by 45% of respondents. Insurance costs and energy efficiency improvements were also highlighted, affecting 41% and 40% of landlords respectively. These escalating expenses are shaping investment decisions across the sector.

One in five landlords (20%) reported selling properties due to rising costs, while 19% have removed properties from the rental market entirely. Delays in upgrades and improvement works were noted by 46% of respondents, raising concerns about potential stagnation in investment within the private rented sector.

Compliance Costs and Regulatory Challenges

The survey also shed light on the financial impact of regulatory changes. The median estimated cost of complying with the Renters’ Rights Act is £5,000, though the average figure stands at £31,411, reflecting significant variation across portfolios. Over the next 12 months, landlords anticipate spending a median of £20,000 on compliance and property upgrades.

James Sproule, UK chief economist at Handelsbanken, commented on the findings, noting that the private rented sector is becoming not only more expensive but also more selective. He emphasised that higher costs and enhanced tenant protections are influencing rent decisions and reshaping landlords’ strategies around tenant risk, affordability, and long-term portfolio planning.

As landlords continue to navigate these challenges, the private rented sector is likely to see further changes in the coming months. Balancing increased costs, regulatory demands, and tenant protections remains a complex task for property investors.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rising costs and evolving regulations are prompting landlords across the UK to reassess their strategies, from rental pricing to tenant selection. According to recent research, a significant proportion of property investors are adapting their approach to navigate the challenges facing the private rented sector.

Rent Increases and Tenant Selection

A recent survey of 200 landlords and property management professionals revealed that 63% have increased rents in response to mounting expenses. Factors such as higher borrowing costs, maintenance outlays, insurance premiums, and compliance requirements have placed considerable pressure on landlords. Additionally, 41% of respondents reported becoming more selective about tenants, focusing on those perceived as lower-risk.

The forthcoming Renters’ Rights Act has further influenced landlords’ decisions, with 59% tightening their tenant selection criteria. Nearly half (44%) are considering accelerating planned rent increases to offset rising costs. These findings suggest that landlords are not only adjusting rental prices but also re-evaluating their approach to tenant risk and long-term tenancy stability.

Cost Pressures and Investment Decisions

Maintenance and repairs emerged as the most significant cost burden, cited by 45% of respondents. Insurance costs and energy efficiency improvements were also highlighted, affecting 41% and 40% of landlords respectively. These escalating expenses are shaping investment decisions across the sector.

One in five landlords (20%) reported selling properties due to rising costs, while 19% have removed properties from the rental market entirely. Delays in upgrades and improvement works were noted by 46% of respondents, raising concerns about potential stagnation in investment within the private rented sector.

Compliance Costs and Regulatory Challenges

The survey also shed light on the financial impact of regulatory changes. The median estimated cost of complying with the Renters’ Rights Act is £5,000, though the average figure stands at £31,411, reflecting significant variation across portfolios. Over the next 12 months, landlords anticipate spending a median of £20,000 on compliance and property upgrades.

James Sproule, UK chief economist at Handelsbanken, commented on the findings, noting that the private rented sector is becoming not only more expensive but also more selective. He emphasised that higher costs and enhanced tenant protections are influencing rent decisions and reshaping landlords’ strategies around tenant risk, affordability, and long-term portfolio planning.

As landlords continue to navigate these challenges, the private rented sector is likely to see further changes in the coming months. Balancing increased costs, regulatory demands, and tenant protections remains a complex task for property investors.

item.title: Property industry urged to respond to government’s call for evidence
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property sector is being called upon to contribute insights to a government consultation that may reshape how property data is managed throughout the home buying and selling process. The Open Property Data Association (OPDA) is encouraging industry professionals to participate in the Department for Business and Trade’s Smart Data initiative, which examines how standardised data sharing could transform multiple sectors, including property.

What the consultation means for property professionals

Running until 1st October 2026, this consultation could influence future regulations and digital frameworks within the property market. Smart Data schemes aim to create secure, standardised methods for sharing information between consumers and businesses. For the housing market, this could streamline transactions by reducing delays caused by fragmented data systems.

The initiative follows the government’s Home Buying and Selling Roadmap, which outlined plans to modernise property transactions through greater digitisation. While the roadmap specifically addressed conveyancing processes, the Smart Data programme has broader implications for the entire property ecosystem.

Why industry participation matters

The OPDA is urging input from across the sector, including:

  • Estate agents
  • Mortgage lenders
  • Conveyancers
  • Surveyors and valuers
  • Insurance providers
  • Property technology firms

Maria Harris, OPDA chair, emphasised the importance of industry engagement: "This consultation presents a crucial opportunity for property professionals to shape future data standards. Too often, policy decisions are made without sufficient sector input, potentially leading to impractical solutions."

Harris acknowledged the challenges posed by multiple digital initiatives in the property space, but stressed that consistent participation remains essential. The development of robust data-sharing frameworks could benefit all parties involved in property transactions by creating more efficient processes and reducing the risk of errors or delays.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, successful implementation of Smart Data principles could eventually lead to smoother transactions with fewer complications. However, the immediate focus remains on gathering professional insights to inform policy development before any changes are implemented.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property sector is being called upon to contribute insights to a government consultation that may reshape how property data is managed throughout the home buying and selling process. The Open Property Data Association (OPDA) is encouraging industry professionals to participate in the Department for Business and Trade’s Smart Data initiative, which examines how standardised data sharing could transform multiple sectors, including property.

What the consultation means for property professionals

Running until 1st October 2026, this consultation could influence future regulations and digital frameworks within the property market. Smart Data schemes aim to create secure, standardised methods for sharing information between consumers and businesses. For the housing market, this could streamline transactions by reducing delays caused by fragmented data systems.

The initiative follows the government’s Home Buying and Selling Roadmap, which outlined plans to modernise property transactions through greater digitisation. While the roadmap specifically addressed conveyancing processes, the Smart Data programme has broader implications for the entire property ecosystem.

Why industry participation matters

The OPDA is urging input from across the sector, including:

  • Estate agents
  • Mortgage lenders
  • Conveyancers
  • Surveyors and valuers
  • Insurance providers
  • Property technology firms

Maria Harris, OPDA chair, emphasised the importance of industry engagement: "This consultation presents a crucial opportunity for property professionals to shape future data standards. Too often, policy decisions are made without sufficient sector input, potentially leading to impractical solutions."

Harris acknowledged the challenges posed by multiple digital initiatives in the property space, but stressed that consistent participation remains essential. The development of robust data-sharing frameworks could benefit all parties involved in property transactions by creating more efficient processes and reducing the risk of errors or delays.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, successful implementation of Smart Data principles could eventually lead to smoother transactions with fewer complications. However, the immediate focus remains on gathering professional insights to inform policy development before any changes are implemented.

item.title: Housing blueprint unveiled ahead of key vote
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Home Builders Federation (HBF) has urged Greater Manchester’s future mayor to harness devolved powers to tackle housing shortages, improve affordability and stimulate home ownership. Their newly published manifesto outlines a strategic approach to accelerate construction and economic growth ahead of the mayoral by-election.

Boosting Housing Delivery and Economic Growth

Greater Manchester has consistently exceeded national housebuilding rates, delivering an average of 10,650 homes annually over the past five years. The HBF highlights that meeting the city region’s annual target of 14,400 homes could generate substantial economic benefits, including:

  • Supporting approximately 50,000 jobs per year
  • Generating £3.2bn in economic activity
  • Securing £650m for affordable housing
  • Investing £90m in infrastructure, including £40m for schools

Despite this progress, affordability pressures persist across all boroughs, with house prices outpacing wage growth. Homeownership with a mortgage in the North West has declined from 39% to 28% of households over two decades, while private renting has more than doubled.

Key Recommendations for the Next Mayor

The HBF’s manifesto proposes several measures to maintain momentum in housing delivery while addressing systemic challenges. Key priorities include:

  • Developing a new Spatial Development Strategy to guide growth
  • Allocating more small sites for SME housebuilders
  • Continuing brownfield development near transport links
  • Collaborating with lenders and government to improve homeownership access

The federation emphasises that strategic planning and devolved decision-making could position Greater Manchester as a national exemplar in addressing housing shortages. However, they note that persistent issues like planning department resourcing and first-time buyer support require both local and national solutions.

With housing completions in Greater Manchester rising 1.3% last year against a 6% national decline, the region demonstrates what targeted policies can achieve. The incoming mayor’s approach to housing could significantly influence whether this positive trend continues.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Home Builders Federation (HBF) has urged Greater Manchester’s future mayor to harness devolved powers to tackle housing shortages, improve affordability and stimulate home ownership. Their newly published manifesto outlines a strategic approach to accelerate construction and economic growth ahead of the mayoral by-election.

Boosting Housing Delivery and Economic Growth

Greater Manchester has consistently exceeded national housebuilding rates, delivering an average of 10,650 homes annually over the past five years. The HBF highlights that meeting the city region’s annual target of 14,400 homes could generate substantial economic benefits, including:

  • Supporting approximately 50,000 jobs per year
  • Generating £3.2bn in economic activity
  • Securing £650m for affordable housing
  • Investing £90m in infrastructure, including £40m for schools

Despite this progress, affordability pressures persist across all boroughs, with house prices outpacing wage growth. Homeownership with a mortgage in the North West has declined from 39% to 28% of households over two decades, while private renting has more than doubled.

Key Recommendations for the Next Mayor

The HBF’s manifesto proposes several measures to maintain momentum in housing delivery while addressing systemic challenges. Key priorities include:

  • Developing a new Spatial Development Strategy to guide growth
  • Allocating more small sites for SME housebuilders
  • Continuing brownfield development near transport links
  • Collaborating with lenders and government to improve homeownership access

The federation emphasises that strategic planning and devolved decision-making could position Greater Manchester as a national exemplar in addressing housing shortages. However, they note that persistent issues like planning department resourcing and first-time buyer support require both local and national solutions.

With housing completions in Greater Manchester rising 1.3% last year against a 6% national decline, the region demonstrates what targeted policies can achieve. The incoming mayor’s approach to housing could significantly influence whether this positive trend continues.

item.title: Estate agent’s survey finds regional divide on annual property tax
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has uncovered a clear regional divide in attitudes towards replacing council tax and stamp duty with an annual property tax. A survey of over 2,200 UK adults, commissioned by estate agency Cavendish, revealed that support for the proposed tax reform varies significantly across the country, with opposition strongest in areas with higher property prices.

Regional Differences in Support

The survey found that northern England and Wales showed the highest levels of support for a single annual property tax. In the North East, 37% of respondents backed the idea, while 35% were in favour in the North West. Wales also recorded relatively strong support, with 33% of respondents agreeing with the proposal. In contrast, London and the South East saw the lowest levels of support, with just 28% and 23% respectively in favour.

Strong opposition was also more prevalent in London and the South East, where 16% of respondents firmly rejected the idea. This compares to 10% in the North West. The North West and Wales also had the highest proportion of respondents who strongly supported the proposal, at 16%, while London and the South East recorded just 7% in this category.

Uncertainty Among Homeowners

The survey highlighted that a significant proportion of homeowners remain undecided on the issue. Nationally, 37% of respondents said they were unsure whether they supported or opposed the proposed tax reform. This suggests that many homeowners may need more information before forming a clear opinion.

David Adams, managing director of Cavendish, commented on the findings, stating: “What is most significant is that more than a third of people still don’t know what they think. That suggests the debate is still in its infancy, and many homeowners will be urgently seeking more information so they can work out the potential financial costs to them.”

Reflecting Regional Property Prices

Simon Leadbetter, founder of The Voice of the Agent and marketing adviser to Cavendish, noted that the regional differences in support mirrored variations in house prices across the UK. “The regional differences are striking. Although this survey wasn’t designed to explain why people hold these views, the pattern mirrors regional house prices,” he said.

This correlation suggests that homeowners in areas with higher property values may be more cautious about a tax system based on property valuations, potentially fearing higher costs. Meanwhile, those in regions with lower property prices might view the proposed annual tax as a fairer alternative to the current system.

As the debate over property tax reform continues, it is clear that homeowners across the UK will be closely monitoring developments. With many still undecided, further clarity on the potential financial implications could play a key role in shaping public opinion.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research has uncovered a clear regional divide in attitudes towards replacing council tax and stamp duty with an annual property tax. A survey of over 2,200 UK adults, commissioned by estate agency Cavendish, revealed that support for the proposed tax reform varies significantly across the country, with opposition strongest in areas with higher property prices.

Regional Differences in Support

The survey found that northern England and Wales showed the highest levels of support for a single annual property tax. In the North East, 37% of respondents backed the idea, while 35% were in favour in the North West. Wales also recorded relatively strong support, with 33% of respondents agreeing with the proposal. In contrast, London and the South East saw the lowest levels of support, with just 28% and 23% respectively in favour.

Strong opposition was also more prevalent in London and the South East, where 16% of respondents firmly rejected the idea. This compares to 10% in the North West. The North West and Wales also had the highest proportion of respondents who strongly supported the proposal, at 16%, while London and the South East recorded just 7% in this category.

Uncertainty Among Homeowners

The survey highlighted that a significant proportion of homeowners remain undecided on the issue. Nationally, 37% of respondents said they were unsure whether they supported or opposed the proposed tax reform. This suggests that many homeowners may need more information before forming a clear opinion.

David Adams, managing director of Cavendish, commented on the findings, stating: “What is most significant is that more than a third of people still don’t know what they think. That suggests the debate is still in its infancy, and many homeowners will be urgently seeking more information so they can work out the potential financial costs to them.”

Reflecting Regional Property Prices

Simon Leadbetter, founder of The Voice of the Agent and marketing adviser to Cavendish, noted that the regional differences in support mirrored variations in house prices across the UK. “The regional differences are striking. Although this survey wasn’t designed to explain why people hold these views, the pattern mirrors regional house prices,” he said.

This correlation suggests that homeowners in areas with higher property values may be more cautious about a tax system based on property valuations, potentially fearing higher costs. Meanwhile, those in regions with lower property prices might view the proposed annual tax as a fairer alternative to the current system.

As the debate over property tax reform continues, it is clear that homeowners across the UK will be closely monitoring developments. With many still undecided, further clarity on the potential financial implications could play a key role in shaping public opinion.

item.title: Planning reform alone will not deliver new homes, ministers warned
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Experts have cautioned that planning reforms alone may not be enough to boost UK housebuilding to government targets, with developers facing mounting financial pressures that could hinder construction. Property consultancy Colliers highlights that alongside planning changes, ministers must address the economic challenges affecting development viability.

Economic hurdles overshadow planning reforms

Andrew White, head of UK residential at Colliers, notes that whilst the new government may bring fresh momentum to planning reforms, the fundamental economics of residential development have shifted. Identifying land and setting housing targets is no longer the primary challenge – restoring favourable conditions for viable construction has become critical.

White explains that slower sales of new-build properties reflect market realities rather than developer reluctance. Housebuilders naturally align construction with demand, meaning build rates slow when sales dip. This isn't indicative of unwillingness to build, but rather a necessary response to market absorption capacity.

Growing cost burdens for developers

Several factors are squeezing development margins according to Colliers' analysis:

  • Rising construction costs across materials and labour
  • More expensive development finance options
  • Prolonged planning delays adding uncertainty
  • Increasing regulatory requirements and compliance costs

White acknowledges that policies like affordable housing quotas, Section 106 agreements, the Community Infrastructure Levy, Biodiversity Net Gain and building safety regulations serve important purposes. However, their combined effect has substantially increased both the cost and complexity of residential projects.

The solution, White suggests, isn't to abandon quality or environmental standards but for policymakers to better understand how these cumulative requirements impact viability. With the right balance, both policy objectives and housing delivery could potentially be achieved.

Broader approach needed for housing targets

Whilst planning reform remains essential – particularly with better-resourced local planning authorities to improve market confidence – Colliers argues it cannot single-handedly deliver the government's housing ambitions. White emphasises that multiple factors need attention:

  • Improving housing affordability to stimulate demand
  • Providing clearer, more consistent policy frameworks
  • Supporting critical infrastructure investment
  • Maintaining realistic assessments of development viability

For homeowners and prospective buyers awaiting more housing supply, these insights suggest that comprehensive solutions beyond planning changes may be needed to significantly boost construction rates. The property market may wish to watch how the new government addresses these complex, interconnected challenges in the coming months.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Experts have cautioned that planning reforms alone may not be enough to boost UK housebuilding to government targets, with developers facing mounting financial pressures that could hinder construction. Property consultancy Colliers highlights that alongside planning changes, ministers must address the economic challenges affecting development viability.

Economic hurdles overshadow planning reforms

Andrew White, head of UK residential at Colliers, notes that whilst the new government may bring fresh momentum to planning reforms, the fundamental economics of residential development have shifted. Identifying land and setting housing targets is no longer the primary challenge – restoring favourable conditions for viable construction has become critical.

White explains that slower sales of new-build properties reflect market realities rather than developer reluctance. Housebuilders naturally align construction with demand, meaning build rates slow when sales dip. This isn't indicative of unwillingness to build, but rather a necessary response to market absorption capacity.

Growing cost burdens for developers

Several factors are squeezing development margins according to Colliers' analysis:

  • Rising construction costs across materials and labour
  • More expensive development finance options
  • Prolonged planning delays adding uncertainty
  • Increasing regulatory requirements and compliance costs

White acknowledges that policies like affordable housing quotas, Section 106 agreements, the Community Infrastructure Levy, Biodiversity Net Gain and building safety regulations serve important purposes. However, their combined effect has substantially increased both the cost and complexity of residential projects.

The solution, White suggests, isn't to abandon quality or environmental standards but for policymakers to better understand how these cumulative requirements impact viability. With the right balance, both policy objectives and housing delivery could potentially be achieved.

Broader approach needed for housing targets

Whilst planning reform remains essential – particularly with better-resourced local planning authorities to improve market confidence – Colliers argues it cannot single-handedly deliver the government's housing ambitions. White emphasises that multiple factors need attention:

  • Improving housing affordability to stimulate demand
  • Providing clearer, more consistent policy frameworks
  • Supporting critical infrastructure investment
  • Maintaining realistic assessments of development viability

For homeowners and prospective buyers awaiting more housing supply, these insights suggest that comprehensive solutions beyond planning changes may be needed to significantly boost construction rates. The property market may wish to watch how the new government addresses these complex, interconnected challenges in the coming months.

item.title: Property industry reacts to latest ONS house price figures
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices saw annual growth of 2.7% in May, with the average property reaching £271,000, according to the latest Office for National Statistics (ONS) data. The figures show a modest monthly increase of 0.3% between April and May 2026, significantly slower than the 1.5% rise recorded during the same period last year. The ONS suggests this cooling reflects base effects from Stamp Duty Land Tax changes introduced in April 2025.

Market resilience amid economic pressures

Industry experts highlight the property market's continued resilience despite economic headwinds. Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, notes the figures remain positive for sellers, though affordability challenges persist for many buyers—particularly first-time purchasers. With inflation data and Bank of England rate decisions looming, alongside Ofgem's upcoming energy price cap review, external factors could influence market confidence in coming months.

The recent political transition has introduced additional uncertainty, with housing policy direction under the new prime minister yet to be clarified. Such ambiguity may temporarily dampen consumer sentiment whilst households await clearer signals about the government's approach to property taxation, planning reforms and support schemes.

A shifting balance between buyers and sellers

Nick Leeming of Jackson-Stops observes the market is becoming more balanced, with buyers demonstrating increased selectivity and sellers adopting more realistic pricing strategies. Well-presented homes aligned with local market conditions continue attracting strong interest, whilst over-ambitious valuations increasingly struggle to gain traction.

Research indicates 42% of delayed movers cite economic uncertainty as a key factor, suggesting stability remains crucial for sustaining transaction volumes. The current environment appears to favour considered purchases based on value and quality rather than urgency, with stock levels providing buyers greater choice than during supply-constrained periods.

As the market adjusts to new economic realities, industry voices emphasise the importance of addressing transaction costs and improving policy clarity to support long-term activity. With borrowing costs and living expenses influencing decisions, the coming months may see continued price moderation alongside selective demand in well-priced market segments.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices saw annual growth of 2.7% in May, with the average property reaching £271,000, according to the latest Office for National Statistics (ONS) data. The figures show a modest monthly increase of 0.3% between April and May 2026, significantly slower than the 1.5% rise recorded during the same period last year. The ONS suggests this cooling reflects base effects from Stamp Duty Land Tax changes introduced in April 2025.

Market resilience amid economic pressures

Industry experts highlight the property market's continued resilience despite economic headwinds. Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, notes the figures remain positive for sellers, though affordability challenges persist for many buyers—particularly first-time purchasers. With inflation data and Bank of England rate decisions looming, alongside Ofgem's upcoming energy price cap review, external factors could influence market confidence in coming months.

The recent political transition has introduced additional uncertainty, with housing policy direction under the new prime minister yet to be clarified. Such ambiguity may temporarily dampen consumer sentiment whilst households await clearer signals about the government's approach to property taxation, planning reforms and support schemes.

A shifting balance between buyers and sellers

Nick Leeming of Jackson-Stops observes the market is becoming more balanced, with buyers demonstrating increased selectivity and sellers adopting more realistic pricing strategies. Well-presented homes aligned with local market conditions continue attracting strong interest, whilst over-ambitious valuations increasingly struggle to gain traction.

Research indicates 42% of delayed movers cite economic uncertainty as a key factor, suggesting stability remains crucial for sustaining transaction volumes. The current environment appears to favour considered purchases based on value and quality rather than urgency, with stock levels providing buyers greater choice than during supply-constrained periods.

As the market adjusts to new economic realities, industry voices emphasise the importance of addressing transaction costs and improving policy clarity to support long-term activity. With borrowing costs and living expenses influencing decisions, the coming months may see continued price moderation alongside selective demand in well-priced market segments.

item.title: Rent growth slows to 3.3% as NRLA warns against rent controls
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK private rental growth has slowed to 3.3% annually according to the latest Office for National Statistics (ONS) data, with average monthly rents now standing at £1,388 across the country. The figures for June 2026 show a continued moderation from the 8.7% growth recorded in October 2024, whilst wages have risen faster than rents at 4.3% over the same period.

Regional rental variations persist

The ONS data reveals significant regional differences in rental costs, with England maintaining the highest average at £1,446 per month. Wales saw the most affordable rents at £843, followed by Northern Ireland (£877) and Scotland (£1,012). Despite these variations, all nations experienced year-on-year increases, continuing the trend of rising housing costs across the UK.

The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) has highlighted that the current slowdown in rental growth coincides with wage increases outpacing housing costs. This comes as some policymakers consider potential rent control measures to address affordability concerns in the private rental sector.

Industry warns against rent controls

Chris Norris, NRLA's chief policy officer, cautioned that introducing rent controls could have unintended consequences: "Evidence from international markets suggests such measures often reduce rental supply and ultimately disadvantage tenants." The association points to previous statements by housing secretary Angela Rayner and minister Matthew Pennycook, who have both expressed concerns about rent controls potentially limiting housing availability.

The NRLA also referenced a Housing, Communities and Local Government Select Committee report which questioned whether stronger rent stabilisation measures would be appropriate, given their potential impact on affordable housing supply. With rental growth already slowing naturally, industry voices argue market-led solutions may prove more effective than government intervention.

As the debate continues, renters and landlords alike may wish to monitor how these economic factors develop, particularly in relation to wage growth and housing policy decisions. The coming months could prove crucial for understanding the long-term trajectory of the UK's rental market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK private rental growth has slowed to 3.3% annually according to the latest Office for National Statistics (ONS) data, with average monthly rents now standing at £1,388 across the country. The figures for June 2026 show a continued moderation from the 8.7% growth recorded in October 2024, whilst wages have risen faster than rents at 4.3% over the same period.

Regional rental variations persist

The ONS data reveals significant regional differences in rental costs, with England maintaining the highest average at £1,446 per month. Wales saw the most affordable rents at £843, followed by Northern Ireland (£877) and Scotland (£1,012). Despite these variations, all nations experienced year-on-year increases, continuing the trend of rising housing costs across the UK.

The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) has highlighted that the current slowdown in rental growth coincides with wage increases outpacing housing costs. This comes as some policymakers consider potential rent control measures to address affordability concerns in the private rental sector.

Industry warns against rent controls

Chris Norris, NRLA's chief policy officer, cautioned that introducing rent controls could have unintended consequences: "Evidence from international markets suggests such measures often reduce rental supply and ultimately disadvantage tenants." The association points to previous statements by housing secretary Angela Rayner and minister Matthew Pennycook, who have both expressed concerns about rent controls potentially limiting housing availability.

The NRLA also referenced a Housing, Communities and Local Government Select Committee report which questioned whether stronger rent stabilisation measures would be appropriate, given their potential impact on affordable housing supply. With rental growth already slowing naturally, industry voices argue market-led solutions may prove more effective than government intervention.

As the debate continues, renters and landlords alike may wish to monitor how these economic factors develop, particularly in relation to wage growth and housing policy decisions. The coming months could prove crucial for understanding the long-term trajectory of the UK's rental market.

item.title: UK annual house price growth slows to 2.7%, ONS data shows
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK house price growth continued to moderate in May, with annual inflation slowing to 2.7% compared to 3.9% the previous month, according to the Office for National Statistics. The average property now stands at £271,000, with regional variations painting a mixed picture across the nations. The slowdown follows last year's Stamp Duty changes in England and Northern Ireland, creating a base effect that has softened annual comparisons.

Regional variations in price movements

The latest figures reveal significant regional disparities in housing market performance. While England saw average prices rise 2.3% annually to £292,000, Wales and Scotland outpaced this growth with increases of 4.2% and 4.4% respectively. Northern Ireland maintained its position as the strongest performer across UK nations, recording 7.4% growth in the first quarter of 2026.

Within England, the North East remained the fastest-growing region despite seeing its annual growth rate nearly halve from 9.7% to 5.9%. At the other end of the spectrum, London prices fell 3.7% year-on-year - the ninth consecutive monthly decline. The capital's downturn was particularly pronounced in inner boroughs, with Westminster and Tower Hamlets experiencing some of the most substantial drops.

Understanding the market slowdown

The cooling market conditions reflect several factors beyond the Stamp Duty base effect. Mortgage affordability pressures continue to influence buyer demand, particularly in higher-value markets like London. Meanwhile, more affordable regions such as the North East, Wales and Scotland appear to be benefiting from relative value and potential lifestyle changes post-pandemic.

Prospective buyers and homeowners might consider that monthly price growth has also slowed, with the 0.3% increase between April and May 2026 comparing unfavourably to the 1.5% rise seen during the same period last year. This gradual deceleration suggests the market is entering a period of stabilisation after the volatility of recent years.

As always with property market trends, local conditions vary considerably and national averages can mask important micro-trends. Those monitoring the housing market may wish to pay particular attention to regional variations when considering their next move.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK house price growth continued to moderate in May, with annual inflation slowing to 2.7% compared to 3.9% the previous month, according to the Office for National Statistics. The average property now stands at £271,000, with regional variations painting a mixed picture across the nations. The slowdown follows last year's Stamp Duty changes in England and Northern Ireland, creating a base effect that has softened annual comparisons.

Regional variations in price movements

The latest figures reveal significant regional disparities in housing market performance. While England saw average prices rise 2.3% annually to £292,000, Wales and Scotland outpaced this growth with increases of 4.2% and 4.4% respectively. Northern Ireland maintained its position as the strongest performer across UK nations, recording 7.4% growth in the first quarter of 2026.

Within England, the North East remained the fastest-growing region despite seeing its annual growth rate nearly halve from 9.7% to 5.9%. At the other end of the spectrum, London prices fell 3.7% year-on-year - the ninth consecutive monthly decline. The capital's downturn was particularly pronounced in inner boroughs, with Westminster and Tower Hamlets experiencing some of the most substantial drops.

Understanding the market slowdown

The cooling market conditions reflect several factors beyond the Stamp Duty base effect. Mortgage affordability pressures continue to influence buyer demand, particularly in higher-value markets like London. Meanwhile, more affordable regions such as the North East, Wales and Scotland appear to be benefiting from relative value and potential lifestyle changes post-pandemic.

Prospective buyers and homeowners might consider that monthly price growth has also slowed, with the 0.3% increase between April and May 2026 comparing unfavourably to the 1.5% rise seen during the same period last year. This gradual deceleration suggests the market is entering a period of stabilisation after the volatility of recent years.

As always with property market trends, local conditions vary considerably and national averages can mask important micro-trends. Those monitoring the housing market may wish to pay particular attention to regional variations when considering their next move.

item.title: Tenant fraud is on the rise, says vast majority of landlords
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A growing number of UK landlords are reporting concerns about tenant fraud, with new research revealing 77% believe such incidents have become more frequent over the past three years. The study by LegalforLandlords highlights financial risks for property owners, with some cases resulting in losses exceeding £5,000.

Rising concerns among landlords

The survey of 1,000 landlords found that 39% consider tenant fraud to have increased significantly, while a further 38% report it has become somewhat more common. These findings follow recent media coverage of serious tenancy fraud cases, including a BBC Panorama investigation into criminal exploitation of rental properties.

One in ten landlords reported direct experience with fraudulent tenants, with the most common issue being deliberate rent avoidance after providing misleading application information (34% of cases). Other frequent problems included properties being used for criminal activity without the landlord's knowledge (19%) and unauthorised subletting arrangements (10%).

Financial impact and resolution challenges

The financial consequences for affected landlords can be substantial. Nearly a quarter (24%) reported losses of £5,000 or more from their most serious incident, while 22% faced costs between £2,500 and £4,999. Only 2% of cases resulted in no financial impact.

Resolution methods varied, with 31% of landlords absorbing the losses completely and 22% pursuing formal eviction proceedings. Alarmingly, 12% of cases remained unresolved at the time of the survey. While most fraud incidents occurred as one-off events, almost a third of affected landlords experienced multiple cases within five years.

Sim Sekhon of LegalforLandlords commented: "The financial impact of tenancy fraud can be severe, whether through misleading affordability details, hidden occupants or criminal exploitation. Landlords often only discover issues after tenancies begin, making resolution more complex and costly."

For property owners, thorough tenant referencing and regular property checks might help mitigate risks. However, the research underscores the importance of vigilance in an increasingly challenging rental market.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A growing number of UK landlords are reporting concerns about tenant fraud, with new research revealing 77% believe such incidents have become more frequent over the past three years. The study by LegalforLandlords highlights financial risks for property owners, with some cases resulting in losses exceeding £5,000.

Rising concerns among landlords

The survey of 1,000 landlords found that 39% consider tenant fraud to have increased significantly, while a further 38% report it has become somewhat more common. These findings follow recent media coverage of serious tenancy fraud cases, including a BBC Panorama investigation into criminal exploitation of rental properties.

One in ten landlords reported direct experience with fraudulent tenants, with the most common issue being deliberate rent avoidance after providing misleading application information (34% of cases). Other frequent problems included properties being used for criminal activity without the landlord's knowledge (19%) and unauthorised subletting arrangements (10%).

Financial impact and resolution challenges

The financial consequences for affected landlords can be substantial. Nearly a quarter (24%) reported losses of £5,000 or more from their most serious incident, while 22% faced costs between £2,500 and £4,999. Only 2% of cases resulted in no financial impact.

Resolution methods varied, with 31% of landlords absorbing the losses completely and 22% pursuing formal eviction proceedings. Alarmingly, 12% of cases remained unresolved at the time of the survey. While most fraud incidents occurred as one-off events, almost a third of affected landlords experienced multiple cases within five years.

Sim Sekhon of LegalforLandlords commented: "The financial impact of tenancy fraud can be severe, whether through misleading affordability details, hidden occupants or criminal exploitation. Landlords often only discover issues after tenancies begin, making resolution more complex and costly."

For property owners, thorough tenant referencing and regular property checks might help mitigate risks. However, the research underscores the importance of vigilance in an increasingly challenging rental market.

item.title: Landlords targeting portfolio growth through refinancing, survey finds
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A significant majority of buy-to-let landlords are looking to refinance their property portfolios within the next year to fund expansion plans, according to new research. The study reveals growing confidence in the rental market despite recent regulatory changes, with many investors actively seeking opportunities beyond traditional southern hotspots.

Refinancing plans signal landlord confidence

Specialist lender Together found that 76% of landlords are considering refinancing their existing properties in the coming 12 months, with over a third describing themselves as "very likely" to take this step. Only 12% of respondents ruled out refinancing altogether. This trend suggests many property investors are looking to release equity from their current holdings to finance further purchases, rather than maintaining their existing portfolios.

The findings indicate sustained optimism about the long-term prospects of the buy-to-let sector, even after the introduction of the Renters' Rights Act. Industry experts view this refinancing activity as a sign that experienced landlords are adapting to market changes while continuing to see value in property investment.

Northern regions attract investor attention

Lending data reveals a noticeable shift in geographical investment patterns, with northern England and Scotland gaining popularity among buy-to-let investors. Between 2020 and 2025, the North West saw the biggest increase in market share for buy-to-let lending, followed by Scotland and Yorkshire. Meanwhile, London and the South East's combined share of lending declined during the same period.

This regional rebalancing appears driven by several factors:

  • More attractive rental yields compared to southern regions
  • Stronger capital growth potential in some northern markets
  • Better affordability for portfolio expansion
  • Sustained tenant demand in key northern cities

Russell Anderson of Together commented: "Investors continue to be drawn to locations where affordability, rental demand and long-term growth prospects remain compelling. The funding data shows a clear concentration of activity across northern England as landlords seek stronger returns."

For those considering refinancing or expanding their property portfolios, comparing mortgage products and seeking professional financial advice could help identify the most suitable options based on individual circumstances and investment goals.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A significant majority of buy-to-let landlords are looking to refinance their property portfolios within the next year to fund expansion plans, according to new research. The study reveals growing confidence in the rental market despite recent regulatory changes, with many investors actively seeking opportunities beyond traditional southern hotspots.

Refinancing plans signal landlord confidence

Specialist lender Together found that 76% of landlords are considering refinancing their existing properties in the coming 12 months, with over a third describing themselves as "very likely" to take this step. Only 12% of respondents ruled out refinancing altogether. This trend suggests many property investors are looking to release equity from their current holdings to finance further purchases, rather than maintaining their existing portfolios.

The findings indicate sustained optimism about the long-term prospects of the buy-to-let sector, even after the introduction of the Renters' Rights Act. Industry experts view this refinancing activity as a sign that experienced landlords are adapting to market changes while continuing to see value in property investment.

Northern regions attract investor attention

Lending data reveals a noticeable shift in geographical investment patterns, with northern England and Scotland gaining popularity among buy-to-let investors. Between 2020 and 2025, the North West saw the biggest increase in market share for buy-to-let lending, followed by Scotland and Yorkshire. Meanwhile, London and the South East's combined share of lending declined during the same period.

This regional rebalancing appears driven by several factors:

  • More attractive rental yields compared to southern regions
  • Stronger capital growth potential in some northern markets
  • Better affordability for portfolio expansion
  • Sustained tenant demand in key northern cities

Russell Anderson of Together commented: "Investors continue to be drawn to locations where affordability, rental demand and long-term growth prospects remain compelling. The funding data shows a clear concentration of activity across northern England as landlords seek stronger returns."

For those considering refinancing or expanding their property portfolios, comparing mortgage products and seeking professional financial advice could help identify the most suitable options based on individual circumstances and investment goals.

item.title: Controversy over Rayner return
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The property sector has responded with cautious optimism to the new government appointments, with Andy Burnham’s elevation to Prime Minister drawing broad support whilst Angela Rayner’s reappointment as Housing Secretary has sparked debate among industry figures.

Mixed reactions to housing leadership

Angela Rayner’s return to the role of Housing Secretary has divided opinion across the property market. Some industry leaders have expressed concerns about potential policy shifts, particularly around private rental sector reforms and housing targets. Others, however, point to her experience in the role and understanding of housing challenges as positive factors during a period of market uncertainty.

The reappointment comes at a critical time for the UK housing market, with affordability pressures, mortgage rate volatility and planning system reforms all requiring urgent attention. Property professionals will be watching closely for early indications of whether Ms Rayner intends to maintain continuity with previous policies or introduce significant changes.

Broad support for new Prime Minister

Andy Burnham’s appointment as Prime Minister has been met with largely positive responses from property experts. Many cite his track record as Mayor of Greater Manchester, where he championed housing initiatives and infrastructure projects, as evidence of his understanding of property market dynamics.

Key areas of interest for the sector include whether the new PM will prioritise:

  • Accelerated housebuilding programmes
  • Reforms to the planning system
  • Support for first-time buyers
  • Measures to address regional housing disparities

The coming weeks are likely to see increased lobbying from property groups as the new government establishes its priorities. With housing remaining a critical issue for many voters, the property sector will be keen to see how these appointments translate into concrete policies affecting homeowners, renters and investors alike.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The property sector has responded with cautious optimism to the new government appointments, with Andy Burnham’s elevation to Prime Minister drawing broad support whilst Angela Rayner’s reappointment as Housing Secretary has sparked debate among industry figures.

Mixed reactions to housing leadership

Angela Rayner’s return to the role of Housing Secretary has divided opinion across the property market. Some industry leaders have expressed concerns about potential policy shifts, particularly around private rental sector reforms and housing targets. Others, however, point to her experience in the role and understanding of housing challenges as positive factors during a period of market uncertainty.

The reappointment comes at a critical time for the UK housing market, with affordability pressures, mortgage rate volatility and planning system reforms all requiring urgent attention. Property professionals will be watching closely for early indications of whether Ms Rayner intends to maintain continuity with previous policies or introduce significant changes.

Broad support for new Prime Minister

Andy Burnham’s appointment as Prime Minister has been met with largely positive responses from property experts. Many cite his track record as Mayor of Greater Manchester, where he championed housing initiatives and infrastructure projects, as evidence of his understanding of property market dynamics.

Key areas of interest for the sector include whether the new PM will prioritise:

  • Accelerated housebuilding programmes
  • Reforms to the planning system
  • Support for first-time buyers
  • Measures to address regional housing disparities

The coming weeks are likely to see increased lobbying from property groups as the new government establishes its priorities. With housing remaining a critical issue for many voters, the property sector will be keen to see how these appointments translate into concrete policies affecting homeowners, renters and investors alike.

item.title: Third of landlords raise rents after RRA
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research from estate agency Chestertons reveals that approximately one in three landlords have increased rental prices following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA). The findings highlight how property owners are responding to recent legislative changes affecting the private rental sector.

Landlords adjust to regulatory changes

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force earlier this year, introduced several protections for tenants including enhanced eviction safeguards and stricter property standards. While designed to improve renters' living conditions, the legislation appears to have prompted some landlords to review their pricing strategies.

Chestertons' data suggests that:

  • 33% of landlords increased rents after the RRA implementation
  • Smaller portfolio owners were more likely to adjust prices than large-scale investors
  • Rent rises were most common in areas with high tenant demand

Potential impacts on the rental market

Industry observers note that rent increases following regulatory changes are not uncommon, as landlords often seek to offset perceived additional costs or risks. However, with many tenants already facing affordability pressures, further rent rises could present challenges.

Prospective renters might consider:

  • Researching local market trends before agreeing new tenancies
  • Negotiating longer fixed-term contracts to lock in current rates
  • Exploring alternative locations where rental growth has been more modest

While the RRA aims to create a fairer rental market, its full effects on both tenants and landlords may take time to become clear. Both parties may benefit from staying informed about their rights and responsibilities under the new legislation.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research from estate agency Chestertons reveals that approximately one in three landlords have increased rental prices following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA). The findings highlight how property owners are responding to recent legislative changes affecting the private rental sector.

Landlords adjust to regulatory changes

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force earlier this year, introduced several protections for tenants including enhanced eviction safeguards and stricter property standards. While designed to improve renters' living conditions, the legislation appears to have prompted some landlords to review their pricing strategies.

Chestertons' data suggests that:

  • 33% of landlords increased rents after the RRA implementation
  • Smaller portfolio owners were more likely to adjust prices than large-scale investors
  • Rent rises were most common in areas with high tenant demand

Potential impacts on the rental market

Industry observers note that rent increases following regulatory changes are not uncommon, as landlords often seek to offset perceived additional costs or risks. However, with many tenants already facing affordability pressures, further rent rises could present challenges.

Prospective renters might consider:

  • Researching local market trends before agreeing new tenancies
  • Negotiating longer fixed-term contracts to lock in current rates
  • Exploring alternative locations where rental growth has been more modest

While the RRA aims to create a fairer rental market, its full effects on both tenants and landlords may take time to become clear. Both parties may benefit from staying informed about their rights and responsibilities under the new legislation.

item.title: Geo-political tensions lead to mortgage rate rises again
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Mortgage rates have seen fresh increases across the UK market as global tensions and financial market movements put upward pressure on borrowing costs. Several major lenders have adjusted their residential and buy-to-let mortgage offerings upwards in recent days, marking another shift in what has been a volatile period for interest rates.

What's driving the latest rate rises?

The current wave of mortgage rate increases appears closely tied to movements in swap rates – the financial instruments lenders use to price fixed-rate mortgages. These have been climbing amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainties and shifting expectations about the Bank of England's monetary policy.

Financial markets remain sensitive to international developments, with concerns about prolonged conflicts and trade disruptions contributing to higher funding costs for lenders. This has translated into more expensive mortgage products reaching the high street, affecting both homebuyers and property investors.

What does this mean for borrowers?

For those currently on variable rate deals or coming to the end of fixed-term agreements, these developments may warrant closer attention to the market. Borrowers might consider:

  • Reviewing their current mortgage terms and any upcoming renewal dates
  • Comparing products across different lenders to find competitive rates
  • Speaking with a mortgage broker about available options

While rates remain below their 2022 peaks, the recent increases serve as a reminder that mortgage costs can change quickly in response to global events and economic conditions. Homeowners approaching the end of fixed deals may wish to begin their remortgaging research earlier than usual to allow time for market comparisons.

As always with financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances could prove valuable when navigating these changing market conditions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Mortgage rates have seen fresh increases across the UK market as global tensions and financial market movements put upward pressure on borrowing costs. Several major lenders have adjusted their residential and buy-to-let mortgage offerings upwards in recent days, marking another shift in what has been a volatile period for interest rates.

What's driving the latest rate rises?

The current wave of mortgage rate increases appears closely tied to movements in swap rates – the financial instruments lenders use to price fixed-rate mortgages. These have been climbing amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainties and shifting expectations about the Bank of England's monetary policy.

Financial markets remain sensitive to international developments, with concerns about prolonged conflicts and trade disruptions contributing to higher funding costs for lenders. This has translated into more expensive mortgage products reaching the high street, affecting both homebuyers and property investors.

What does this mean for borrowers?

For those currently on variable rate deals or coming to the end of fixed-term agreements, these developments may warrant closer attention to the market. Borrowers might consider:

  • Reviewing their current mortgage terms and any upcoming renewal dates
  • Comparing products across different lenders to find competitive rates
  • Speaking with a mortgage broker about available options

While rates remain below their 2022 peaks, the recent increases serve as a reminder that mortgage costs can change quickly in response to global events and economic conditions. Homeowners approaching the end of fixed deals may wish to begin their remortgaging research earlier than usual to allow time for market comparisons.

As always with financial decisions, seeking professional advice tailored to individual circumstances could prove valuable when navigating these changing market conditions.

item.title: Landlord company registrations up 1,700%
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The number of landlords operating through limited companies has grown by a staggering 1,700% since the turn of the century, according to recent industry analysis. This dramatic rise signals a fundamental shift in how buy-to-let properties are being managed across the UK's private rental sector.

The move towards professional property management

Experts suggest this trend reflects the increasing professionalisation of the rental market, with more landlords choosing to operate through corporate structures rather than as individual investors. The growth in company registrations comes as the sector faces tighter regulation and tax changes that have made incorporation more attractive for some property owners.

Several factors appear to be driving this shift:

  • Changes to mortgage interest tax relief for individual landlords
  • The ability to offset more expenses against profits when operating through a company
  • Potential inheritance tax benefits of holding property in a corporate structure
  • Growing regulatory requirements making professional management more appealing

What this means for the rental market

The surge in company-owned rental properties could have significant implications for both tenants and the wider housing market. Professional landlords may offer more standardised tenancy agreements and property maintenance, potentially improving conditions for renters. However, some analysts warn this trend could also lead to further consolidation in the sector, with smaller landlords potentially being priced out.

For those considering entering the buy-to-let market, the shift towards company structures may influence their approach. Mortgage products for limited companies have become more widely available in recent years, though borrowers might find that interest rates and fees differ from personal buy-to-let mortgages.

The private rental sector continues to evolve rapidly, with this latest data highlighting how tax and regulatory changes are reshaping landlord behaviour. Both existing and prospective property investors may wish to seek professional financial advice to understand how these trends could affect their individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The number of landlords operating through limited companies has grown by a staggering 1,700% since the turn of the century, according to recent industry analysis. This dramatic rise signals a fundamental shift in how buy-to-let properties are being managed across the UK's private rental sector.

The move towards professional property management

Experts suggest this trend reflects the increasing professionalisation of the rental market, with more landlords choosing to operate through corporate structures rather than as individual investors. The growth in company registrations comes as the sector faces tighter regulation and tax changes that have made incorporation more attractive for some property owners.

Several factors appear to be driving this shift:

  • Changes to mortgage interest tax relief for individual landlords
  • The ability to offset more expenses against profits when operating through a company
  • Potential inheritance tax benefits of holding property in a corporate structure
  • Growing regulatory requirements making professional management more appealing

What this means for the rental market

The surge in company-owned rental properties could have significant implications for both tenants and the wider housing market. Professional landlords may offer more standardised tenancy agreements and property maintenance, potentially improving conditions for renters. However, some analysts warn this trend could also lead to further consolidation in the sector, with smaller landlords potentially being priced out.

For those considering entering the buy-to-let market, the shift towards company structures may influence their approach. Mortgage products for limited companies have become more widely available in recent years, though borrowers might find that interest rates and fees differ from personal buy-to-let mortgages.

The private rental sector continues to evolve rapidly, with this latest data highlighting how tax and regulatory changes are reshaping landlord behaviour. Both existing and prospective property investors may wish to seek professional financial advice to understand how these trends could affect their individual circumstances.

item.title: One in four not signed up to MTD
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

With the first Making Tax Digital (MTD) filing deadline approaching, new figures reveal that around 25% of eligible landlords and sole traders still haven’t registered for the scheme. The government initiative, which requires digital record-keeping and quarterly submissions for income tax, comes into full effect from April 2026 for those with annual business or property income above £50,000.

What landlords and sole traders need to know

Making Tax Digital represents a significant shift in how tax reporting works in the UK. Under the scheme:

  • Eligible taxpayers must maintain digital records of income and expenses
  • Quarterly updates must be submitted to HMRC using compatible software
  • A final declaration replaces the traditional Self Assessment tax return

For buy-to-let landlords, this change affects those with rental income above the threshold. The phased rollout means those with income between £30,000 and £50,000 will need to comply from April 2027.

Why early preparation matters

With one in four yet to register, HMRC has emphasised the importance of acting now to avoid last-minute complications. Property owners and self-employed individuals might consider:

  • Researching MTD-compatible accounting software options
  • Reviewing current record-keeping systems for digital compatibility
  • Allowing time to familiarise themselves with the new submission process
