Experts suggest that leaving registration too close to the deadline could lead to processing delays or potential penalties for late submissions. The transition period offers an opportunity to test systems and resolve any technical issues well in advance.

As the MTD rollout continues, staying informed about requirements and deadlines remains crucial for affected taxpayers. Those yet to register may find it beneficial to begin the process sooner rather than later to ensure a smooth transition to the new digital tax system.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

With the first Making Tax Digital (MTD) filing deadline approaching, new figures reveal that around 25% of eligible landlords and sole traders still haven’t registered for the scheme. The government initiative, which requires digital record-keeping and quarterly submissions for income tax, comes into full effect from April 2026 for those with annual business or property income above £50,000.

What landlords and sole traders need to know

Making Tax Digital represents a significant shift in how tax reporting works in the UK. Under the scheme:

  • Eligible taxpayers must maintain digital records of income and expenses
  • Quarterly updates must be submitted to HMRC using compatible software
  • A final declaration replaces the traditional Self Assessment tax return

For buy-to-let landlords, this change affects those with rental income above the threshold. The phased rollout means those with income between £30,000 and £50,000 will need to comply from April 2027.

Why early preparation matters

With one in four yet to register, HMRC has emphasised the importance of acting now to avoid last-minute complications. Property owners and self-employed individuals might consider:

  • Researching MTD-compatible accounting software options
  • Reviewing current record-keeping systems for digital compatibility
  • Allowing time to familiarise themselves with the new submission process

Experts suggest that leaving registration too close to the deadline could lead to processing delays or potential penalties for late submissions. The transition period offers an opportunity to test systems and resolve any technical issues well in advance.

As the MTD rollout continues, staying informed about requirements and deadlines remains crucial for affected taxpayers. Those yet to register may find it beneficial to begin the process sooner rather than later to ensure a smooth transition to the new digital tax system.

item.title: MTD Deadline 7 August 2026: What UK Landlords Must File Now
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords with qualifying property income should mark 7th August 2026 in their diaries, as this date represents the first major quarterly submission deadline under HMRC's Making Tax Digital (MTD) initiative for the 2026/27 tax year. The deadline covers the period from 6th April to 5th July 2026, requiring digital record-keeping and submission through compatible software.

Understanding the MTD Requirements

For landlords falling under MTD rules, this isn't simply a reminder but the first mandatory quarterly submission under the new digital tax system. The requirements apply to those with annual property income above £50,000 (for those already in the scheme) or £30,000 (for those being brought into scope). Key aspects include:

  • Maintaining digital records of all property income and expenses
  • Using MTD-compatible software to submit quarterly updates
  • Providing a final declaration after the tax year ends
  • Keeping records for at least five years after the relevant tax year

Preparing for the Transition

Landlords approaching the threshold or already within scope may wish to consider several preparatory steps:

  • Evaluating current record-keeping systems and transitioning to digital methods
  • Researching and selecting HMRC-approved MTD software solutions
  • Familiarising themselves with the quarterly submission process
  • Considering professional accounting support if managing complex portfolios

Whilst the 2026 deadline may seem distant, implementing these changes gradually could help avoid last-minute complications. Many accounting software providers now offer specific MTD solutions tailored for property investors.

Landlords with questions about their MTD obligations might find it helpful to consult HMRC guidance or seek advice from a qualified tax professional, particularly if managing multiple properties or complex financial arrangements. The shift to digital tax reporting represents a significant change in how landlords manage their tax affairs, making early preparation potentially valuable.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords with qualifying property income should mark 7th August 2026 in their diaries, as this date represents the first major quarterly submission deadline under HMRC's Making Tax Digital (MTD) initiative for the 2026/27 tax year. The deadline covers the period from 6th April to 5th July 2026, requiring digital record-keeping and submission through compatible software.

Understanding the MTD Requirements

For landlords falling under MTD rules, this isn't simply a reminder but the first mandatory quarterly submission under the new digital tax system. The requirements apply to those with annual property income above £50,000 (for those already in the scheme) or £30,000 (for those being brought into scope). Key aspects include:

  • Maintaining digital records of all property income and expenses
  • Using MTD-compatible software to submit quarterly updates
  • Providing a final declaration after the tax year ends
  • Keeping records for at least five years after the relevant tax year

Preparing for the Transition

Landlords approaching the threshold or already within scope may wish to consider several preparatory steps:

  • Evaluating current record-keeping systems and transitioning to digital methods
  • Researching and selecting HMRC-approved MTD software solutions
  • Familiarising themselves with the quarterly submission process
  • Considering professional accounting support if managing complex portfolios

Whilst the 2026 deadline may seem distant, implementing these changes gradually could help avoid last-minute complications. Many accounting software providers now offer specific MTD solutions tailored for property investors.

Landlords with questions about their MTD obligations might find it helpful to consult HMRC guidance or seek advice from a qualified tax professional, particularly if managing multiple properties or complex financial arrangements. The shift to digital tax reporting represents a significant change in how landlords manage their tax affairs, making early preparation potentially valuable.

item.title: Lettings platform launches post-Renters’ Rights Act insurance products
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

As the rental market adapts to the Renters’ Rights Act, lettings platform Goodlord has partnered with Elevate Specialty to introduce new insurance products tailored to the evolving sector. The offerings include landlord protection and tenant income cover, designed to address the challenges of longer tenancies and updated possession rules.

New protections for landlords and tenants

The collaboration has produced two key products embedded within Goodlord’s platform. For landlords, a rent guarantee insurance provides cover against rental income loss and legal expenses – particularly valuable as tenancy agreements become more complex under recent reforms. Meanwhile, Goodlord Rent & Bills offers tenants income protection to help maintain rent and utility payments during periods when they’re unable to work.

These solutions have been developed specifically with the Renters’ Rights Act and Consumer Duty regulations in mind. Mickey Attia of Elevate Specialty noted: “The rental market is changing fast, and insurance needs to keep up. We’ve focused on early resolution and practical outcomes rather than bureaucratic delays.”

Simplifying protection in a changing market

By integrating these services directly into their platform, Goodlord aims to make essential protections more accessible. The company’s insurance director Oli Sherlock explained: “Landlords and agents want solutions that reflect how the market operates today. Embedding these services creates simpler, more relevant protection throughout the tenancy lifecycle.”

The move comes as industry participants adjust to significant regulatory changes. Landlords may find the new insurance products particularly useful given the act’s modifications to possession processes and tenancy durations. Similarly, tenants facing greater job market uncertainty might consider income protection as a way to safeguard their housing stability.

As the rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations could play an important role in balancing the needs of landlords and tenants while complying with new legal requirements.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

As the rental market adapts to the Renters’ Rights Act, lettings platform Goodlord has partnered with Elevate Specialty to introduce new insurance products tailored to the evolving sector. The offerings include landlord protection and tenant income cover, designed to address the challenges of longer tenancies and updated possession rules.

New protections for landlords and tenants

The collaboration has produced two key products embedded within Goodlord’s platform. For landlords, a rent guarantee insurance provides cover against rental income loss and legal expenses – particularly valuable as tenancy agreements become more complex under recent reforms. Meanwhile, Goodlord Rent & Bills offers tenants income protection to help maintain rent and utility payments during periods when they’re unable to work.

These solutions have been developed specifically with the Renters’ Rights Act and Consumer Duty regulations in mind. Mickey Attia of Elevate Specialty noted: “The rental market is changing fast, and insurance needs to keep up. We’ve focused on early resolution and practical outcomes rather than bureaucratic delays.”

Simplifying protection in a changing market

By integrating these services directly into their platform, Goodlord aims to make essential protections more accessible. The company’s insurance director Oli Sherlock explained: “Landlords and agents want solutions that reflect how the market operates today. Embedding these services creates simpler, more relevant protection throughout the tenancy lifecycle.”

The move comes as industry participants adjust to significant regulatory changes. Landlords may find the new insurance products particularly useful given the act’s modifications to possession processes and tenancy durations. Similarly, tenants facing greater job market uncertainty might consider income protection as a way to safeguard their housing stability.

As the rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations could play an important role in balancing the needs of landlords and tenants while complying with new legal requirements.

item.title: Conveyancing platform donates record amount to charity partners
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing platform Conveybuddy has reported its highest-ever quarterly charitable donation, with £9,747 distributed to three UK charities between April and June this year. The record contribution comes as more mortgage advisers use the platform to process conveyancing cases, with each completed transaction generating up to £5 for good causes.

How the charitable initiative works

The platform operates a unique giving model where:

  • For every completed conveyancing case, Conveybuddy donates up to £5 to charity
  • Advisers using the platform can select which of the three partner charities receives their contribution
  • Donations are distributed quarterly to the Motor Neurone Disease Association, Rainbows Hospice and Food Bank Aid

This quarter's record donation follows increased adviser registrations and transaction volumes on the platform. Since launching the initiative, Conveybuddy has maintained its commitment to supporting charities that make a tangible difference across the UK.

A growing impact through conveyancing

Harpal Singh, Conveybuddy's chief executive, commented: "From day one, we wanted our platform to have a meaningful charitable dimension – one where advisers could directly influence which causes benefit from their work. Seeing these donations grow alongside our user base shows how the property sector can create positive change."

The platform's model demonstrates how mortgage and conveyancing services can incorporate charitable giving without affecting costs for homebuyers. Advisers using the system continue their normal workflow while automatically generating donations through completed transactions.

For homeowners going through the property purchase process, working with advisers who use charitable platforms like Conveybuddy could be a way to indirectly support good causes. However, borrowers might still wish to compare conveyancing services based on factors like fees, speed and customer service when making their selection.

As the platform grows, its charitable contributions may continue to increase – potentially benefiting more UK charities in future quarters. The latest donation highlights how innovative models in the property sector can create financial support for vital causes while maintaining efficient service for clients.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing platform Conveybuddy has reported its highest-ever quarterly charitable donation, with £9,747 distributed to three UK charities between April and June this year. The record contribution comes as more mortgage advisers use the platform to process conveyancing cases, with each completed transaction generating up to £5 for good causes.

How the charitable initiative works

The platform operates a unique giving model where:

  • For every completed conveyancing case, Conveybuddy donates up to £5 to charity
  • Advisers using the platform can select which of the three partner charities receives their contribution
  • Donations are distributed quarterly to the Motor Neurone Disease Association, Rainbows Hospice and Food Bank Aid

This quarter's record donation follows increased adviser registrations and transaction volumes on the platform. Since launching the initiative, Conveybuddy has maintained its commitment to supporting charities that make a tangible difference across the UK.

A growing impact through conveyancing

Harpal Singh, Conveybuddy's chief executive, commented: "From day one, we wanted our platform to have a meaningful charitable dimension – one where advisers could directly influence which causes benefit from their work. Seeing these donations grow alongside our user base shows how the property sector can create positive change."

The platform's model demonstrates how mortgage and conveyancing services can incorporate charitable giving without affecting costs for homebuyers. Advisers using the system continue their normal workflow while automatically generating donations through completed transactions.

For homeowners going through the property purchase process, working with advisers who use charitable platforms like Conveybuddy could be a way to indirectly support good causes. However, borrowers might still wish to compare conveyancing services based on factors like fees, speed and customer service when making their selection.

As the platform grows, its charitable contributions may continue to increase – potentially benefiting more UK charities in future quarters. The latest donation highlights how innovative models in the property sector can create financial support for vital causes while maintaining efficient service for clients.
